transformer.definition: A neural network architecture for sequence transduction that relies entirely on attention mechanisms instead of recurrence or convolutions.

bert.definition: BERT is a bidirectional Transformer-based language representation model pre-trained with masked language modeling and next sentence prediction for fine-tuning on downstream NLP tasks.

t5.definition: A unified text-to-text transfer learning framework that casts every NLP problem as text input to text output and trains a single model across tasks.

paper:2001.08361.canonical: Empirical Scaling Laws for Language Mode
paper:2001.08361.definition: It is unclear how language model loss depends on model size, dataset size, and compute, and how to allocate a fixed compute budget optimally.

realm.definition: A retrieval-augmented language model pre-training framework that learns a latent knowledge retriever and uses retrieved documents during pre-training, fine-tuning, and inference.

dense passage retriever (dpr).definition: A dual-encoder dense retrieval model that embeds questions and passages into a shared vector space and retrieves relevant passages by maximizing inner products.

longformer.definition: A modified Transformer architecture with a sparse attention mechanism that scales linearly with sequence length for processing long documents.

led.definition: A Longformer-based encoder-decoder model designed for long-document sequence-to-sequence tasks such as summarization.

colbert.definition: A BERT-based ranking model that uses contextualized late interaction to efficiently compute query-document relevance for retrieval.

rag.definition: A retrieval-augmented generation framework that combines a pre-trained seq2seq generator with a dense retriever over Wikipedia and jointly fine-tunes them end-to-end for knowledge-intensive generation.

gpt 3.definition: An autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters that performs a wide range of NLP tasks in a few-shot setting without gradient updates or task-specific fine-tuning.

switch transformer.definition: A simplified mixture-of-experts transformer that routes each input token to a single expert to enable sparse, scalable training with reduced communication and computation costs.

lora.definition: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that freezes pretrained model weights and injects trainable low-rank decomposition matrices into Transformer layers to adapt large language models.

flan.definition: A pretrained language model instruction-tuned on many NLP datasets phrased as natural language instructions to improve zero-shot performance on unseen tasks.

cot.definition: A few-shot prompting method that includes intermediate natural-language reasoning steps between the input and output to elicit complex reasoning in large language models.

instructgpt.definition: A family of GPT-3 models fine-tuned with supervised demonstrations and reinforcement learning from human feedback to better follow user instructions.

self consistency.definition: A decoding strategy for chain-of-thought prompting that samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the most consistent final answer by marginalizing over the sampled paths.

chinchilla.definition: A compute-optimal 70B-parameter language model trained with substantially more data to maximize performance under a fixed compute budget.

palm.definition: A 540-billion-parameter densely activated Transformer language model trained to study the effects of scaling on few-shot learning and broad language capabilities.

least to most prompting.definition: A two-stage prompting strategy that first decomposes a complex problem into simpler subproblems and then solves them sequentially using earlier subproblem answers as context.

flashattention.definition: An IO-aware exact attention algorithm that uses tiling and kernel fusion to minimize GPU memory traffic between HBM and SRAM.

block sparse flashattention.definition: A sparse attention variant built on FlashAttention that exploits block sparsity to further reduce memory access and speed up attention.

emergent abilities of large language mod.canonical: emergent abilities of large language models
emergent abilities of large language mod.definition: Abilities that are absent in smaller-scale models but present in larger-scale models, and therefore cannot be predicted by extrapolating smaller-model performance.

react.definition: A prompting paradigm that interleaves verbal reasoning traces with task-specific actions to improve language model problem solving.

constitutional ai.definition: A training framework that uses a written constitution of principles plus AI self-critiques and AI preference judgments to train a more harmless assistant with minimal human supervision.

hyde.definition: A zero-shot dense retrieval method that generates a hypothetical document from a query with an instruction-following language model and then embeds it with a contrastive encoder for retrieval.

self instruct.definition: A semi-automated bootstrapping framework that improves instruction-following by generating synthetic instructions and instances from a language model and using them to finetune the same model.

replug.definition: A retrieval-augmented language modeling framework that augments a frozen black-box language model by prepending retrieved documents and optionally ensembles multiple retrieved contexts in parallel.

replug lsr.definition: A training scheme that uses a black-box language model’s scores to supervise and improve the retriever.

toolformer.definition: A language model trained in a self-supervised way to decide which external APIs to call, when to call them, what arguments to pass, and how to integrate the results into next-token prediction.

llama.definition: A collection of open foundation language models trained on publicly available data and optimized for strong performance at different inference budgets.

reflexion.definition: A framework that improves language agents by converting task feedback into verbal self-reflection stored in episodic memory instead of updating model weights.

hugginggpt.definition: An LLM-powered agent that uses ChatGPT as a controller to plan tasks, select expert models from Hugging Face, execute them, and synthesize the results for complex AI requests.

self refine.definition: An iterative self-refinement algorithm that improves an LLM's initial output by repeatedly generating self-feedback and using that feedback to refine the output.

generative agents.definition: Computational agents that simulate believable human behavior by storing, reflecting on, and retrieving memories to plan and act over time.

rankgpt.definition: A prompting and distillation framework that uses large language models to rank passages by directly generating permutations of candidate passages.

plan and solve (ps) prompting.definition: A zero-shot prompting strategy that first asks the model to devise a plan to break a problem into subtasks and then solve the subtasks according to that plan.

ps+ prompting.definition: An extended Plan-and-Solve prompting strategy that adds more detailed instructions to improve reasoning quality and reduce calculation errors.

flare.definition: A forward-looking active retrieval augmented generation method that iteratively predicts the next sentence, retrieves documents using that prediction when token confidence is low, and regenerates the sentence with the retrieved evidence.

tot.definition: A language model inference framework that organizes coherent intermediate reasoning units as a tree and uses search over them for deliberate problem solving.

rewrite retrieve read.definition: A retrieval-augmented LLM framework that rewrites the input query before retrieval to better align the search request with the needed knowledge and improve downstream reading performance.

qlora.definition: An efficient finetuning method that backpropagates through a frozen 4-bit quantized pretrained language model into Low Rank Adapters to enable low-memory LLM finetuning without performance loss.

guanaco.definition: A family of chatbot models finetuned with QLORA that achieves strong Vicuna benchmark performance.

voyager.definition: An LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent for Minecraft that autonomously explores, acquires skills, and makes new discoveries using code-based actions and a growing skill library.

direct preference optimization.definition: A reinforcement-learning-free method for training language models from human preferences by directly optimizing a policy with a binary classification objective.

llama 2.definition: A collection of pretrained and fine-tuned large language models ranging from 7 billion to 70 billion parameters.

llama 2 chat.definition: A fine-tuned version of Llama 2 optimized for dialogue use cases.

metagpt.definition: A multi-agent meta-programming framework that encodes software-development standardized operating procedures into coordinated LLM agent workflows.

autogen.definition: An open-source framework for building LLM applications with customizable, conversable agents that coordinate through multi-agent conversations.

lats.definition: A general language-agent framework that uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to combine reasoning, acting, planning, external feedback, and self-reflection for improved decision-making.

self rag.definition: A retrieval-augmented generation framework that trains a single language model to decide when to retrieve, critique its own outputs, and generate with reflection tokens for improved factuality and controllability.

jina embeddings v2.definition: An open-source text embedding model that extends BERT with bidirectional ALiBi to encode sequences up to 8192 tokens for high-quality document embeddings.

mamba.definition: A simplified end-to-end neural network architecture that integrates selective state space models for linear-time sequence modeling without attention or MLP blocks.

selective state space models.definition: State space models whose parameters are conditioned on the input to enable content-based selection and selective propagation or forgetting of information along the sequence.

proposition indexing.definition: A dense retrieval approach that indexes a corpus using propositions, which are atomic, self-contained factoid units, instead of passages or sentences.

paper:2312.10997.definition: Large language models suffer from hallucination, outdated knowledge, and non-transparent reasoning, and existing RAG research lacks a systematic synthesis and evaluation-focused overview.

crag.definition: A plug-and-play corrective retrieval-augmented generation framework that self-corrects poor retrieval results to improve robustness and document utilization.

raptor.definition: A retrieval and indexing system that recursively embeds, clusters, and summarizes text chunks into a tree of multi-level abstractions for retrieval-augmented generation.

lumberchunker.definition: An LLM-driven dynamic text segmentation method that iteratively identifies content shift points to create semantically independent chunks of variable size.

gutenqa.definition: A benchmark of 3,000 needle-in-a-haystack question-answer pairs derived from 100 public domain narrative books from Project Gutenberg for evaluating retrieval and RAG chunking methods.

late chunking.definition: A chunking method that embeds an entire long document first and applies chunking only after the transformer encoder, before mean pooling, so each chunk embedding retains full document context.

meta chunking.definition: A lightweight chunking framework for RAG that combines uncertainty-based boundary detection with dynamic merging and information compensation to produce more coherent text chunks.
